one_liner: A 6-month-old Ashkenazi Jewish infant is brought in for loss of previously acquired motor milestones and an exaggerated startle response to sounds.

stage2.hpi_extension: The baby sat without support at 4 months and babbled but over the last two months has become floppy, lost the ability to sit, no longer tracks faces, and is increasingly irritable. Parents note a dramatic startle to loud noises (hyperacusis) and two brief generalized tonic-clonic seizures this week. Feeding has become difficult due to poor head control. No fevers, no vomiting, no infections.
stage2.pmh: Uncomplicated pregnancy and term delivery. Normal newborn screen.
stage2.family_history: Both parents are Ashkenazi Jewish. A maternal cousin died in early childhood of an unknown neurologic disease. Parents declined preconception carrier screening.
stage2.social_history: Lives with both parents; no tobacco, no toxins in the home.
stage2.physical_exam: General: alert but hypotonic infant with poor head control. HEENT: cherry-red spot on the macula bilaterally on funduscopy. Neuro: hyperreflexia at the patellae, sustained clonus, exaggerated startle to clap. Abdomen: no hepatosplenomegaly (important negative). Skin: no rash, no dysmorphic features.

lab_results.result: Beta-hexosaminidase A (Hex A) enzyme activity markedly decreased in serum leukocytes. HEXA gene sequencing confirms two pathogenic variants (compound heterozygote), consistent with the common Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutation.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Deficient Hex A confirms Tay-Sachs disease. GM2 ganglioside accumulates in neuronal lysosomes, causing progressive neurodegeneration. Carrier screening in Ashkenazi Jewish couples can prevent affected pregnancies.

lab_results.result: MRI brain: diffuse cerebral and cerebellar atrophy with symmetric T2 hyperintensity of the basal ganglia and thalami. No hydrocephalus. No focal mass lesion.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Bilateral thalamic and basal ganglia signal changes with cortical atrophy are characteristic of GM2 gangliosidosis and help distinguish lysosomal storage disorders from infectious encephalitis.

lab_results.result: Electron microscopy of rectal/conjunctival biopsy: neurons distended with whorled membranous cytoplasmic bodies (onion-skin lysosomal inclusions).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Lamellar cytoplasmic inclusions reflect accumulated GM2 ganglioside within lysosomes and are pathognomonic for Tay-Sachs disease.

stage5.final_clue: The family is counseled that there is no disease-modifying therapy. Supportive care is provided for feeding, seizures, and respiratory complications. Genetic counseling is arranged for future pregnancies, with discussion of prenatal Hex A enzyme testing and preimplantation genetic diagnosis.

debrief.classic_presentation: Tay-Sachs disease is an autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disease that classically presents in infants of Ashkenazi Jewish descent between 3 and 6 months of age with loss of motor milestones, hypotonia, an exaggerated startle response to sound, and a cherry-red spot on the macula. Crucially there is no hepatosplenomegaly, which distinguishes Tay-Sachs from other sphingolipidoses such as Niemann-Pick and Gaucher disease. Affected children progress to blindness, seizures, and death by age 3 to 5.
debrief.pathophysiology: Tay-Sachs is caused by autosomal recessive mutations in the HEXA gene on chromosome 15, which encodes the alpha subunit of beta-hexosaminidase A. Deficient Hex A leads to accumulation of GM2 ganglioside in neuronal lysosomes, particularly in the CNS and retina, producing neuronal swelling, dysfunction, and death. The characteristic cherry-red macula reflects preservation of the red foveal choroid surrounded by pale, lipid-laden retinal ganglion cells.
debrief.treatment_overview: There is no effective disease-modifying treatment. Management is supportive: anticonvulsants for seizures, gastrostomy for feeding, and respiratory care. Prevention is based on preconception carrier screening in Ashkenazi Jewish populations and prenatal testing; preimplantation genetic diagnosis is an option for at-risk couples.
high_yield_pearls: Tay-Sachs = hexosaminidase A deficiency leading to GM2 ganglioside accumulation; Tay-SaX = TAy-SaX (no organomegaly).
Cherry-red spot on the macula occurs in Tay-Sachs, Niemann-Pick, and central retinal artery occlusion — use the presence or absence of hepatosplenomegaly to differentiate.
Tay-Sachs has no hepatosplenomegaly; Niemann-Pick and Gaucher do.
Classic buzzwords: Ashkenazi Jewish infant, exaggerated startle, hyperreflexia, progressive neurodegeneration.
Electron microscopy shows onion-skin / whorled lysosomal inclusions of undegraded GM2 ganglioside.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Biochemistry chapter, 'Lysosomal storage diseases — Sphingolipidoses' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Biochemistry chapter, 'Autosomal recessive disorders' (Tay-Sachs disease, HEXA).
